From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Winter festival

For other uses, see [Yule (disambiguation)](/wiki/Yule_(disambiguation) "Yule (disambiguation)").

This article is about the pagan festival and its modern revivals. For the Christian festival, see [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas").

**This article contains [Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_alphabet "Gothic alphabet") characters.** Without proper [rendering support](/wiki/Help:Multilingual_support#Gothic "Help:Multilingual support"), you may see [question marks, boxes, or other symbols](/wiki/Specials_(Unicode_block)#Replacement_character "Specials (Unicode block)") instead of letters.

**Yule** ([Old English](/wiki/Old_English_language "Old English language"): *ġēol*, [Old Norse](/wiki/Old_Norse_language "Old Norse language"): *jól*) is a winter festival and time of the year historically observed by [heathen](/wiki/Germanic_paganism "Germanic paganism") [Germanic peoples](/wiki/Germanic_peoples "Germanic peoples") that was later merged with the festival of [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas") during the process of [Christianisation](/wiki/Christianisation_of_the_Germanic_peoples "Christianisation of the Germanic peoples"). The oldest accounts describe two Yule-months around the [winter solstice](/wiki/Winter_solstice "Winter solstice") in early, and sometimes explicitly heathen, [Germanic calendars](/wiki/Early_Germanic_calendars "Early Germanic calendars").

Later, and more extensive, [Old Norse](/wiki/Old_Norse "Old Norse") sources also describe a Yule festival occurring during this period, which possibly began on **Hǫkunótt** and continued for several days. During it, feasting, ceremonial drinking and [oath swearing](/wiki/Heitstrenging "Heitstrenging") was central, and plays and games likely took place. Tales set at this time frequently feature supernatural beings visiting halls and farms, such as trolls and undead [draugar](/wiki/Draugar "Draugar"). Such traditions closely resemble folk practices and beliefs from the modern period such as [julebukking](/wiki/Julebukking "Julebukking") and the [Wild Hunt](/wiki/Wild_Hunt "Wild Hunt"), which may have partly heathen roots.

Because of the blending of the festival period and Christmas, Yule and its cognates are still used to refer to Christmas and the season of [Christmastide](/wiki/Christmastide "Christmastide") in English and some other [Northern European](/wiki/Northern_European "Northern European") languages, including [Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_(language) "Swedish (language)") and [Finnish](/wiki/Finnish_(language) "Finnish (language)"). In addition to this, adherents of some [new religious movements](/wiki/New_religious_movements "New religious movements"), such as [Modern Germanic paganism](/wiki/Modern_Germanic_paganism "Modern Germanic paganism"), celebrate Yule as an independent festival to Christmas.

## Etymology and derived terms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yule&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology and derived terms")]

The modern English noun 'Yule' descends from [Middle English](/wiki/Middle_English_language "Middle English language"): *ȝol* (alternative forms include *ȝeol* and *yoole*), in turn from [Old English](/wiki/Old_English_language "Old English language"): *ġēol*, earlier *geoh(h)ol*, *geh(h)ol*, and *geóla*, sometimes pluralised. The Old English term is thought to derive from a [Proto-Germanic](/wiki/Proto-Germanic "Proto-Germanic") word such as \**jehwlą-* or \**iehwulo*.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOED_Online2022-1)[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELiberman2008-2) Other Old English forms of the word 'Yule' were *iūla* and *giuli*.[[3]](#cite_note-Karasawa-3)

Various theories have been put forward on the meaning and origin of the Proto-Germanic root of 'Yule'. Scholars including [Jacob Grimm](/wiki/Jacob_Grimm "Jacob Grimm") have proposed a now widespread, but largely rejected, idea, that it is related to 'wheel', in reference to the completing of a solar cycle at the winter solstice. In support of this, it has been noted that [runic calendars](/wiki/Runic_calendar "Runic calendar") often represent the Christmas celebration with a wheel.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELiberman2008-2)[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENordberg2006121–122-4) It has been noted, however, that this in Old English is *hweol*, and there is no clear reason to connect it to *geol* ('Yule'). A more plausible etymology is to link it to [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *jocus*, the ancestor of [English](/wiki/English_language "English language"): jokecode: eng promoted to code: en . In the ancient period and Middle Ages, 'joke' referred to "entertainment" or "festivity". 'Yule' could therefore be interpreted as having originally meant a "period of joy and public celebration".[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELiberman2008-2) Despite these efforts, the ultimate origin of \**jehwlą-*, and related terms, remains unclear.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELiberman2008-2)[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENordberg2006121–122-4)[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOED_Online2022-1)

'Yule' is cognate with [Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_language "Gothic language"): 𐌾𐌹𐌿𐌻𐌴𐌹𐍃 (*jiuleis*), [Old Norse](/wiki/Old_Norse "Old Norse"): *[jól](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/j%C3%B3l#Old_Norse "wikt:jól")*, and its descendants [Icelandic](/wiki/Icelandic_language "Icelandic language"), [Faroese](/wiki/Faroese_language "Faroese language") and Norwegian [Nynorsk](/wiki/Nynorsk "Nynorsk") *[jól](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/j%C3%B3l#Icelandic "wikt:jól")*, and [Danish](/wiki/Danish_language "Danish language"), [Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"), and [Norwegian Bokmål](/wiki/Bokm%C3%A5l "Bokmål") *jul*.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworthToller1898424-5)[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoad1996550-6)[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOrel2003205-7)[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEjol-Bokmålsordboka-8)[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOED_Online2022-1)[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELiberman2008-2)[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESvensk_ordbok_-_jul-9) The Proto-Germanic root was also loaned into the Finnic languages, giving rise to [Finnish](/wiki/Finnish_language "Finnish language"): *juhla*. Later, a North Germanic word for Yule (such as *jól*) was loaned again, giving *joulu*. This possibly occurred through [Northern Sami](/wiki/Northern_Sami_language "Northern Sami language"): *juowˈlâ* as an intermediate with the same Germanic root.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEjuhla-10)[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEjoulu-11) 'Yule' and its modern cognates typically denote [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas") and the [period around it](/wiki/Christmas_period "Christmas period"), with a slight exception being [Finnish](/wiki/Finnish_language "Finnish language"): *juhla*, which can have that meaning but normally carries the more general sense of "celebration".[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworthToller1898424-5)[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoad1996550-6)[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOrel2003205-7)[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEjol-Bokmålsordboka-8)[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOED_Online2022-1) It remains uncertain if there are any [Indo-European](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages") cognates that are not derived from Proto-Germanic, though numerous speculative attempts have been made.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarnhart1995896-12) It has been suggested that [Old French](/wiki/Old_French_language "Old French language"): *jolif* (later, [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *joli*), which was borrowed into English in the 14th century as 'jolly', may itself borrowed from [Old Norse](/wiki/Old_Norse_language "Old Norse language"): *jól* (with the Old French suffix *-if*; compare Old French *[aisif](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/aisif#Old_French "wikt:aisif")* "easy", Modern French *festif* = *fest* "feast" + *[-if](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/-if#Old_French "wikt:-if")*).[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERey20121806ab-13)[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECNRTL-14)[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoad1993672-15)
But the *Oxford English Dictionary* sees this explanation for *jolif* as unlikely.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOED_Online-jolly-16) The French word is first attested in the Anglo-Norman *[Estoire des Engleis](/wiki/Estoire_des_Engleis "Estoire des Engleis")* ("History of the English People"), written by [Geoffrey Gaimar](/wiki/Geoffrey_Gaimar "Geoffrey Gaimar") between 1136 and 1140.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECNRTL-14)

In addition to denoting [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas") and the period around it, 'Yule' is also used for a heathen midwinter festival celebrated by Scandinavians, and likely other Germanic peoples, before their Christianisation.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJól-17)[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENiles2013122-18)[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESimek2007379-19)[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOrchard1997187-20) In scholarly usage, the same English term is also applied as an umbrella label for pre-Christian Germanic midwinter observances, including Old Norse *[jól](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/j%C3%B3l#Old_Norse "wikt:jól")*, which can denote the midwinter season itself.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETurvillePetre196425–30-21)[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrice201490–94-22)[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESimek2007180–181''Jól''-23) This association with [Old Norse religion](/wiki/Old_Norse_religion "Old Norse religion") is reflected in terms derived from *[jól](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/j%C3%B3l#Old_Norse "wikt:jól")* such as *jólnar*, used in the poetry of [Eyvindr skáldaspillir](/wiki/Eyvindr_sk%C3%A1ldaspillir "Eyvindr skáldaspillir") to refer to the [gods](/wiki/%C3%86sir "Æsir").[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDriscoll199582-24)[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESimek2007379–380-25) The singular form *Jólnir* ("Yule man") is also one of the many names of Odin.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESimek2007379–380-25)[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELindow200128-26)[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeide202491-27) While the 12th century Old Norse *Ágrip* describes *[jól](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/j%C3%B3l#Old_Norse "wikt:jól")* as coming from *Jólnir*, the opposite is true.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDriscoll199582-24)[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESimek2007379–380-25) Other Old Norse terms derived from *[jól](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/j%C3%B3l#Old_Norse "wikt:jól")* include *jólaǫl* ("Yule ale"), *jólagjǫf* ("Yule gift"), *jólaaptann* ("Yule-eve") and *jóladrykkja* ("Yule drinking").[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEjólaöl-28)[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEjólagjöf-29)[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEjólaaptann-30)[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEjóladrykkja-31) Some compound nouns derived from "Yule" in English are first attested long after Christianisation, including *Yuletide* ("Yule-time"; c. 1475), *yule-candle* (1808), *yule-game* (1611) and *yule-log* (1725).[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPage2020-32)

## Yule months

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yule&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Yule months")]

See also: [Early Germanic calendars](/wiki/Early_Germanic_calendars "Early Germanic calendars")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/De_Mensibus_Anglorum_in_Cotton_MS_Vespasian_B_VI_%282%29.jpg/500px-De_Mensibus_Anglorum_in_Cotton_MS_Vespasian_B_VI_%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:De_Mensibus_Anglorum_in_Cotton_MS_Vespasian_B_VI_(2).jpg)

Section of *De Mensibus Anglorum* in *The Reckoning of Time* describing *giuli*, *[Cotton MS Vespasian B VI](/wiki/Cotton_manuscripts "Cotton manuscripts")*.[[note 1]](#cite_note-34)

Yule is attested early in the history of the Germanic peoples, and over a wide geographical range; the earliest of such records is a [Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_language "Gothic language") calendar, found in the c. 350 CE *[Codex Ambrosianus](/wiki/Codices_Ambrosiani "Codices Ambrosiani")*, that mentions the month *fruma jiuleis* ("the month before the Yule-month"), implying a subsequent unrecorded \**jiuleis* ("Yule month").[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENordberg2006148-35)

Two adjacent months named after Yule are recorded by the English historian [Bede](/wiki/Bede "Bede") in *[The Reckoning of Time](/wiki/The_Reckoning_of_Time "The Reckoning of Time")*, dated to c. 726 CE. Bede writes that before his time, the [heathen Anglo-Saxons](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_paganism "Anglo-Saxon paganism") had reckoned their months based on the phases of the moon, and that they had called both December and January *[Giuli](/wiki/%C4%A0%C4%93olamona%C3%BE "Ġēolamonaþ")* (the months of Yule).[[35]](#cite_note-Bede-Willis-1999-36) A 10th century English text refers to these two months as *se ǽrra Geola* and *se æftera Geola*, "the earlier" and "the latter Yule month".[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENordberg200621,_147–148-37)[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBedeChapter_15._The_English_Months-33) Bede further writes that the heathen English started their year on 25 December, when Christians celebrate the birth of Jesus, and says that they called this *[Modranecht](/wiki/Modranecht "Modranecht")* ("Mothers' night").[[35]](#cite_note-Bede-Willis-1999-36) This day likely fell between the two months of *giuli*.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENordberg200666-38) Bede further says the night got its name from the ceremonies he believed they performed then; the mothers are typically interpreted as goddesses, possibly the *[dísir](/wiki/D%C3%ADsir "Dísir")*.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBedeChapter_15._The_English_Months-33)[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOrchard1997115-39)

Old Norse sources also record two Yule months: *Jólmánuðr* ("Yule month") and *Ýlir*, a term with the same root that is likely pre-Christian.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENordberg20064,21-22-40)[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECelander19558-41) The Icelandic *[Bókarbót](/w/index.php?title=B%C3%B3karb%C3%B3t&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bókarbót (page does not exist)")*, usually dated to around 1220, places *Ýlir* from the middle of November to the middle of December in the Julian calendar, and *Jólmánuðr* following afterwards into the middle of January.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENordberg200621-22-42) After the official [Christianisation of Scandinavia](/wiki/Christianisation_of_Scandinavia "Christianisation of Scandinavia"), the Church gradually introduced the Julian calendar, with it being largely established by the middle of the 1100s.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENordberg200624-43) Nonetheless, a lunisolar time-reckoning was practiced in Sweden as late as the early 1900s, where the *Jultungel* ("Yule moon") was the moon that was seen during [Epiphany](/wiki/Epiphany_(holiday) "Epiphany (holiday)") and the [Dísting](/w/index.php?title=D%C3%ADsting&action=edit&redlink=1 "Dísting (page does not exist)") market was held on the following full moon.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENordberg2006116-44)

Given the large geographic area over which there is evidence for moon-based calendars with two Yule months, it has been suggested that they originated substantially earlier than their first attestations, when the relevant Germanic peoples lived closer together. This may be before the migrations of the Goths from the Baltic coast, and possibly the [Angles from southern Scandinavia to Britain](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_settlement_of_Britain "Anglo-Saxon settlement of Britain"). An early dating would further support the development of the calendar in a heathen context, before the adoption of Christianity.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENordberg200622-45)

## Timing of heathen Yule celebrations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yule&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Timing of heathen Yule celebrations")]

The exact timing of the pre-Christian Yule celebrations is unclear and debated among scholars. The earliest record of a seasonal celebration in Scandinavia is given by the 6th century historian [Procopius of Caesaria](/wiki/Procopius_of_Caesaria "Procopius of Caesaria") who writes of the [Heruli](/wiki/Heruli "Heruli") who lived in "[Thule](/wiki/Thule "Thule")" (likely around modern day Norway). He describes how the sun is not seen there for 40 nights in winter and that their greatest festival of the year is celebrated 20 days after the [winter solstice](/wiki/Winter_solstice "Winter solstice"), when the sun appears again. This would be mid January in the Gregorian calendar.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunnell2000121–122-46)[[note 2]](#cite_note-47) Scholars since the 17th century have suggested this is describing heathen Yule celebrations. The nature of Procopius' account, including the possibility that he is describing [Sámi](/wiki/S%C3%A1mi "Sámi") rather than Germanic peoples, complicates interpretations of the source.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENordberg2006101-48)

[Bede](/wiki/Bede "Bede") in the 8th century says that the two [Anglo-Saxon](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon "Anglo-Saxon") Yule months (*Giuli*) are linked to the winter solstice. He writes that they "derive their name from the day when the Sun turns back [and begins] to increase", with one month before the solstice and one following it.[[35]](#cite_note-Bede-Willis-1999-36)[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBedeChapter_15._The_English_Months-33) In surviving Anglo-Saxon calendars, the winter solstice was generally deemed to be December 25, following the [Julian calendar](/wiki/Julian_calendar "Julian calendar").[[47]](#cite_note-Karasawa36-49)[[48]](#cite_note-Parker-50) According to Andreas Nordberg, an issue with this is that the heathen Anglo-Saxons used a lunisolar calendar, which Bede describes, in which each month lasted a full waxing and waning of the moon. The two Anglo-Saxon Yule months would therefore move relative to the winter solstice and it could not always fall in the middle of them.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENordberg200663-51)

In Old Norse sources, the only exact timing of the [Nordic](/wiki/Norsemen "Norsemen") Yule (*Jól*) is found in *Hákonar saga góða*. It describes how the Nordic heathen Yule began on *hǫkunótt*, which is equated with *miðsvetrarnótt* ("Midwinter Night"), and continued for three days.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENordberg2006102,_119-52) Although the English *[midwinter](/wiki/Midwinter "Midwinter")* referred to the time of the winter solstice[[47]](#cite_note-Karasawa36-49)[[48]](#cite_note-Parker-50) the Scandinavian Midwinter Night was about one month after the winter solstice.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENordberg2006155–156-53)[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeimskringla97–98Hákonar_saga_góða,_chapter_13-54)[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESaga_Hákonar_GóðaChapter_15-55) Winter in Scandinavia is deemed to last longer than in southern Germanic regions. In Old Norse sources that divided the year into two seasons, winter was deemed to begin with the "[Winter Nights](/wiki/Winter_Nights "Winter Nights")" in mid-October and to end in mid-April.[[54]](#cite_note-56) *Hǫkunótt* only occurs in this passage, and is alternatively spelt *hǫggunótt* in *[Fríssbók](/wiki/Fr%C3%ADssb%C3%B3k "Fríssbók")*.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENordberg2006119-57) The etymology of *hǫkunótt* is not clear but various interpretations have been put forward, including relating it to the verb *hǫggva* ("to hew", "to slaughter") in reference to the sacrifice of animals at the *[blót](/wiki/Bl%C3%B3t "Blót")* that took place at that time.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHöku-nótt-58)[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENordberg2006119-57)

Scholars have argued that the Nordic heathen Yule feast took place at the winter solstice, despite Snorri Sturluson not placing it then.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESundqvist2015370-59)[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENordberg2006123-60) Andreas Nordberg proposes, instead, that the Nordic heathen Yule was celebrated on the [full moon](/wiki/Full_moon "Full moon") of the [lunar month](/wiki/Lunar_month "Lunar month") following the winter solstice (the lunar month starting on the first new moon after the solstice). This could range from 5 January at the earliest to 2 February at the latest in the Gregorian calendar. Nordberg places the Nordic Midwinter Nights on 19 to 21 January in the Gregorian calendar, falling roughly in the middle of his range of Yule dates. In addition to Snorri's account, Nordberg's dating is consistent with the account of the great blót at [Lejre](/wiki/Lejre "Lejre") by [Thietmar of Merseburg](/wiki/Thietmar_of_Merseburg "Thietmar of Merseburg").[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENordberg2006155–156-53) This interpretation has received scholarly support.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESundqvist2015323-61)

## Historical Nordic traditions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yule&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Historical Nordic traditions")]

### *Blót*, feasting and drinking

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yule&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Blót, feasting and drinking")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Reconstruction_of_the_Taplow_Drinking_Horn_in_the_British_Museum.jpg/250px-Reconstruction_of_the_Taplow_Drinking_Horn_in_the_British_Museum.jpg)](/wiki/File:Reconstruction_of_the_Taplow_Drinking_Horn_in_the_British_Museum.jpg)

Reconstruction of a [Taplow drinking horn](/wiki/Taplow_drinking_horn "Taplow drinking horn"), [British Museum](/wiki/British_Museum "British Museum").

In medieval Scandinavia, *[blót](/wiki/Bl%C3%B3t "Blót")* often took place during Yule celebrations, whereby animals were sacrificed to the gods and their meat cooked and shared among those present. This was typically accompanied by ceremonial drinking of ale or mead.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMagnell2019303-62)[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESteinbock2014134-63)

Communal eating was central to Yule, which is seen in the use of *jól* in [skaldic poetry](/wiki/Skaldic_poetry "Skaldic poetry") to mean 'feast' in the [kenning](/wiki/Kenning "Kenning") for 'battle': *[hugins](/wiki/Huginn "Huginn") jól* ('a raven's (Yule) feast').[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVigfússon1874326-64)[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESundqvist2015370-59)[[note 3]](#cite_note-66) *Jólaveizlur* ("Yule-feasts") are also widely attested in medieval Old Norse accounts of pre-Christian celebrations,[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESundqvist2015320–321-67)[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJólaveizla-68) including the Yule-feast of King [Halfdanr svarti](/wiki/Halfdanr_svarti "Halfdanr svarti") in his [eponymous saga](/wiki/Saga_H%C3%A1lfdanar_svarta "Saga Hálfdanar svarta"), where all the food and ale was made to disappear by a [Sámi](/wiki/S%C3%A1mi "Sámi") man.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeimskringlaHálfdanar_saga_svarta,_chapters_5_&_8-69) Another notable example is in *[Hákonar saga góða](/wiki/H%C3%A1konar_saga_g%C3%B3%C3%B0a "Hákonar saga góða")*, where [Hákon the Good](/wiki/H%C3%A1kon_the_Good "Hákon the Good") is forced to take part in [blót](/wiki/Bl%C3%B3t "Blót")-feasts in [Mære](/wiki/M%C3%A6re "Mære") and [Lade](/wiki/Lade,_Trondheim "Lade, Trondheim"), including eating meat from the [sacrificed horses](/wiki/Horses_in_Germanic_paganism "Horses in Germanic paganism"), as part of his responsibilities as a king.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeimskringlaHákonar_saga_goðar,_chapter_18-70)[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESundqvist2015325,_344-71) Yule has further been identified by some scholars with the *[álfablót](/wiki/%C3%81lfabl%C3%B3t "Álfablót")* ("[elf](/wiki/Elf "Elf")-blót") recorded in *[Ólafs saga helga](/wiki/%C3%93lafs_saga_helga "Ólafs saga helga")*, though this has been rejected by others as *álfablót* were likely held in autumn, not winter.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESundqvist2015369–370-72)

Drinking ceremonies also likely took place at Yule, consistent with their prominence in [Germanic paganism](/wiki/Germanic_paganism "Germanic paganism") more widely.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESundqvist2015352-73)[[note 4]](#cite_note-75) Early evidence of this is found in [Þorbjǫrn hornklofi](/wiki/%C3%9Eorbj%C7%ABrn_hornklofi "Þorbjǫrn hornklofi")'s *[Haraldskvæði](/wiki/Haraldskv%C3%A6%C3%B0i "Haraldskvæði")*, written around 900 CE, where [Harald Fairhair](/wiki/Harald_Fairhair "Harald Fairhair") "drinks Yule", or "drinks to Yule":

| [Old Norse](/wiki/Old_Norse "Old Norse") text | Translation[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunnell199528-76) |
| --- | --- |
| *Uti vill jól drekka,*  *ef skal einn ráða*  *fylkir enn framlyndi,*  *ok Freys leik heyja;*  *ungr leiddisk eldvelli*  *ok inni (at) sitja,*  *varma dyngu*  *eða vöttu dúns fulla* | He wants to drink (to?) Yule outside  if he can decide alone,  the fame-seeking ruler  and perform Frey’s *leikr*;  the young man was tired  of the fireside and sitting indoors  in the warm women’s room  or down-filled cushions |

Here, "drinking Yule" seems to be synonymous with celebrating it.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECelander195511,13-77) At the feast in Mære, King Hákon also drinks toasts that were poured for him, consistent with the importance of ritual drinking at Yule.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeimskringlaHákonar_saga_goðar,_chapter_18-70)

The importance of drinking at Yule is likely reflected in surviving customs after Christianisation. *[Den ældre Gulathings-Lov](/wiki/Den_%C3%A6ldre_Gulathings-Lov "Den ældre Gulathings-Lov")*, an early law code from Norway, imposes punishments for incorrect preparation of ale for *jól* ('Yule' or 'Christmas'). The required practices include brewing in groups, unless one lives very remotely, and hallowing the ale to thank [Christ](/wiki/Christ "Christ") and [St Mary](/wiki/St_Mary "St Mary") *[til árs. oc til friðar](/wiki/Til_%C3%A1rs._oc_til_fri%C3%B0ar "Til árs. oc til friðar")* ("for prosperity and peace") - a ritual formula that likely originated in heathen contexts.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECelander195511,_13–16-78)[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESundqvist2015357-358-79) This has been interpreted as an example of missionaries Christianising a heathen custom by replacing heathen gods with Christian figures.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECelander195516-80) Furthermore, *Hákonar saga góða* says that in King Hákon's effort to Christianise Norway, he shifted Yule to Christmas time. He also imposed a fine on anyone who did not have a measure of ale at Yule (estimated to be around 16.2 litres), and made it law for the holiday to continue as long as the ale lasted.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeimskringla97Hákonar_saga_goðar,_chapter_13-81)

### *Heitstrenging*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yule&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Heitstrenging")]

The swearing of solemn vows, *[Heitstrenging](/wiki/Heitstrenging "Heitstrenging")*, on Yule-Eve are attested in *[Helgakviða Hjörvarðssonar](/wiki/Helgakvi%C3%B0a_Hj%C3%B6rvar%C3%B0ssonar "Helgakviða Hjörvarðssonar")* and *[Hervarar saga ok Heiðreks](/wiki/Hervarar_saga_ok_Hei%C3%B0reks "Hervarar saga ok Heiðreks")*, the first of which reads:

| [Old Norse](/wiki/Old_Norse "Old Norse") text[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHelgakviða_Hjörvarðssonar_ON-82) | Bellows translation[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBellows1936Helgakviða_Hjörvarðssonar,_prose_between_stanzas_30_&_31-83) |
| --- | --- |
| *Heðinn fór einn saman heim ór skógi jólaaftan ok fann trollkonu. Sú reið vargi ok hafði orma at taumum ok bauð fylgð sína Heðni. "Nei," sagði hann. Hon sagði: "Þess skaltu gjalda at bragarfulli." Um kveldit óru heitstrengingar. Var fram leiddr sónargöltr. Lögðu menn þar á hendr sínar ok strengðu menn þá heit at bragarfulli.* | Hethin was coming home alone from the forest one Yule-eve, and found a troll-woman; she rode on a wolf, and had snakes in place of a bridle. She asked Hethin for his company. "Nay," said he. She said, "Thou shalt pay for this at the king's toast." That evening the great vows (*heitstrengingar*) were taken; the sacred boar (*[sonargöltr](/wiki/Sonarg%C3%B6ltr "Sonargöltr")*) was brought in, the men laid their hands thereon, and took their vows at the king's toast (*bragafull*). |

*Hervarar saga ok Heiðreks* similarly takes place on Yule-Eve and describes people placing their hands on a pig referred to as a *[sonargöltr](/wiki/Sonarg%C3%B6ltr "Sonargöltr")* before swearing solemn oaths at the *[bragafull](/wiki/Bragafull "Bragafull")* ('toast', 'libation').[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESundqvist2015500-84)[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBragarfull-85) Some manuscripts of the text explicitly refer to the pig as holy, that it was devoted to [Freyr](/wiki/Freyr "Freyr") and that after the oath-swearing it was sacrificed at a *[blót](/wiki/Bl%C3%B3t "Blót")*.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKovářová2011195–196-86)

### Games and performances

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yule&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Games and performances")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Fighting_Mimes_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/250px-Fighting_Mimes_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fighting_Mimes_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

Fresco of two warriors, one of which with the head of a dog, on the ceiling of [St. Sophia Cathedral](/wiki/Saint_Sophia_Cathedral,_Kyiv "Saint Sophia Cathedral, Kyiv") in [Kyiv](/wiki/Kyiv "Kyiv").

Games during Yule are widely attested in the Old Norse record, including [glíma](/wiki/Gl%C3%ADma "Glíma") in *[Króka-Refs saga](/wiki/Kr%C3%B3ka-Refs_saga "Króka-Refs saga")*, [knattleikr](/wiki/Knattleikr "Knattleikr") in *[Hálfdanar saga Eysteinssonar](/wiki/H%C3%A1lfdanar_saga_Eysteinssonar "Hálfdanar saga Eysteinssonar")* and [tug-of-war](/wiki/Tug-of-war "Tug-of-war") in [Hjálmþés saga ok Ölvis](/wiki/Hj%C3%A1lm%C3%BE%C3%A9s_saga_ok_%C3%96lvis "Hjálmþés saga ok Ölvis").[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunnell199534-87) Performances and plays, possibly such as those depicted on the [Sutton Hoo helmet](/wiki/Sutton_Hoo_helmet "Sutton Hoo helmet"), may also have been connected to the pre-Christian festival.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESundqvist2015352–353-88)

The earlier discussed stanza from *[Haraldskvæði](/wiki/Haraldskv%C3%A6%C3%B0i "Haraldskvæði")* uses the term "[Frey](/wiki/Frey "Frey")’s *leikr*", which may be a Yuletide ritual dedicated to the god.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunnell199528-76) *Leikr*, and the verb *leika*, typically refer to an activity such as a "game", "dramatic performance" or "dance" .[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunnell199528-29-89) The phrase is, however, also used in the later *[Ragnars saga loðbrókar](/wiki/Ragnars_saga_lo%C3%B0br%C3%B3kar "Ragnars saga loðbrókar")* as a kenning for battle and this may be the meaning intended in *Haraldskvæði*.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunnell199528-76)[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFulk201291-90) This interpretation has alternatively been suggested to be a misunderstanding of the earlier meaning.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunnell199528-76) Furthermore, *leikr* is cognate with [Old English](/wiki/Old_English_language "Old English language"): *lāc*, which has diverse meanings from 'play', 'sacrifice', 'gift' and 'battle'.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESundqvist2015352-73)

A further possible attestation is the *[gothikon](/w/index.php?title=Gothikon&action=edit&redlink=1 "Gothikon (page does not exist)")*, a performance described in the 10th century [Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine "Byzantine") *[Book of ceremonies](/wiki/De_Ceremoniis "De Ceremoniis")* as taking place on [Twelfth Night](/wiki/Twelfth_Night_(holiday) "Twelfth Night (holiday)"). The performance consisted of two groups of men, each performing a circle dance, one circle inside the other, while accompanied by two pairs of men wearing masks and skins. Throughout the dance, the performers hit their shields with sticks, while shouting "Toúl" ("τούλ").[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAxton197433–34-91)[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunnell199573–74-92)[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESandin2009121–122-93) This word's meaning is debated but is widely identified as a misspelling or misunderstanding of *jól* ("Yule"), and the performance as part of a Yule tradition.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunnell199573–74-92)[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESandin2009126-94) While the text identifies the dancers as [Goths](/wiki/Goths "Goths"), these are not elsewhere attested in [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople") after around 500. It is possible they were instead Scandinavians, possibly from [Gotland](/wiki/Gotland "Gotland") or [Götaland](/wiki/G%C3%B6taland "Götaland"), who are widely attested as [Varangians](/wiki/Varangians "Varangians").[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunnell199573–74-92)[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESandin2009126-94)[[note 5]](#cite_note-95) This description has been noted for its similarity to a [fresco](/wiki/Fresco "Fresco") of two fighting warriors on the ceiling of [St Sophia Cathedral](/wiki/Saint_Sophia_Cathedral_in_Kyiv "Saint Sophia Cathedral in Kyiv") in [Kyiv](/wiki/Kyiv "Kyiv"). One has a round shield and an [Dane axe](/wiki/Dane_axe "Dane axe"), likely intended of a visual marker of their identity as a Varangian and a Scandinavian elite. The other fighter has the head of a dog, possibly a mask like in the *Book of ceremonies*, that may derive from heathen religious dramas connected to *berserkir*.[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBandlien2022''The_Varangian_and_the_Dog-Head_Warrior_in_Kiev''-96) Notably, two felt animal masks dating to the 10th century have been discovered in [Hedeby](/wiki/Hedeby "Hedeby").[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBandlien2022''The_Varangian_and_the_Dog-Head_Warrior_in_Kiev''-96)[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunnell199575–76-97)

### Supernatural visitations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yule&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Supernatural visitations")]

Yule visits from supernatural beings, such as *[berserkir](/wiki/Berserkir "Berserkir")*, trolls and undead *[draugar](/wiki/Draugar "Draugar")*, are widely attested across Northern Europe in modern folklore.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOrchard1997182–183,_187-98) Given their early attestations, without clear Christian underpinnings, it has been proposed such ideas were present before Christianisation.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunnell200462-99) An early account of these visits is in *[Eyrbyggja saga](/wiki/Eyrbyggja_saga "Eyrbyggja saga")*, in which the farm on [Breiðafjörður](/wiki/Brei%C3%B0afj%C3%B6r%C3%B0ur "Breiðafjörður") in [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland "Iceland") is taken over by ghosts of those who have died both at land and sea.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunnell200451–52-100) This motif is also seen with the arrival of the half-*[etayn](/wiki/Etayn "Etayn")* Green Knight in the c. 14th century *[Sir Gawain and the Green Knight](/wiki/Sir_Gawain_and_the_Green_Knight "Sir Gawain and the Green Knight")* and in later tales, such as the balads of [Åsmund Frægdegjeva](/w/index.php?title=%C3%85smund_Fr%C3%A6gdegjeva&action=edit&redlink=1 "Åsmund Frægdegjeva (page does not exist)") and [Steinfinn Fefinnson](/w/index.php?title=Steinfinn_Fefinnson&action=edit&redlink=1 "Steinfinn Fefinnson (page does not exist)").[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGeeraert201873–74,_83–84-101)[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeaver2024298-102)[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECumpstey20161–16-103)

Often the visits result in conflict, as in *[Grettis saga](/wiki/Grettis_saga "Grettis saga")*, in which Grettir beheads the undead shepherd Glamur, who had been haunting the area at Yule.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrettis_sagaChapters_32-35-104)[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunnell200451–52-100) Another Yule, a troll-woman who was attacking a hall is driven back to her waterfall home by Grettir, who then kills her.[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrettis_sagaChapters_64-66-105)[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunnell200451–52-100) This section of the saga closely resembles tales such as the Old English poem *[Beowulf](/wiki/Beowulf "Beowulf")* and is likely part of a shared tradition.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunnell200451–52-100) Strong similarities are also seen between these tales and folktales recorded in the 18th century in Iceland, such as *[Sagan af Grimi Skeljungsbana](/w/index.php?title=Sagan_af_Grimi_Skeljungsbana&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sagan af Grimi Skeljungsbana (page does not exist)")*.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunnell200460-106) Changes are seen over time, however, with an overall shift from the hero defeating the visitor with his own strength, to relying on external factors like calling on [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus") or the light of dawn. Furthermore, there is a general increase in the number of invading beings and in Iceland, they are *[huldufólk](/wiki/Hulduf%C3%B3lk "Huldufólk")* or elves, rather than the older trolls and ghosts.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunnell200462,_71-107)

#### The Wild Host

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yule&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: The Wild Host")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Gotland-Bunge_Museum_Hof_18.Jhdt._16.jpg/250px-Gotland-Bunge_Museum_Hof_18.Jhdt._16.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gotland-Bunge_Museum_Hof_18.Jhdt._16.jpg)

Julbock costume, [Bungemuseet](/w/index.php?title=Bungemuseet&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bungemuseet (page does not exist)"), [Gotland](/wiki/Gotland "Gotland").

A specific type of supernatural visitations that have been connected to the heathen Yule and the winter solstice is the [Wild Host (or Wild Hunt)](/wiki/Wild_Hunt "Wild Hunt").[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESimek2007180–181,_379–380-108)[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOrchard1997187-20) This is a constructed academic category for a diverse collection of traditions found throughout North-Western Europe for groups of supernatural beings travelling across the landscape, often in winter or around Christmas.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeide201978-109) Names for these customs vary greatly between regions, including the Norwegian *Oskoreia*, *Juleskreia* ("the yule host"), *Julereia* ("The yule/Christmas ride") and *Julesveinane* ("the Yule lads"). Traits of the variants are largely independent of the name of the host or its leader.[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeide201976–78-110) The beings of the host are often noisy and harmful dead, but may also include goats (in particular *[Julebukkane](/wiki/Julbock "Julbock")* ("the Yule billy-goats"). According to folktales, as they travel through the land, they invade farms in their way, stealing Christmas food, Christmas ale, and sometimes even people or horses. Taken horses are almost ridden to death while people may be thrown down after a time, either where they were abducted or far away.[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunnell200466,_77-111) In the 20th century, tar crosses were painted on farmhouse doors in Western Norway for protection against them.[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunnell200466-112)

The host is variously led by a diverse range of figures, for example the figures from [Germanic heroic legend](/wiki/Germanic_heroic_legend "Germanic heroic legend"), [Sigurd Svein](/wiki/Sigurd_Svein "Sigurd Svein") and [Guro Rysserova](/wiki/Guro_Rysserova "Guro Rysserova").[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeide201976–77-113)[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunnell200466-112) In several regions of northern Europe, [Odin](/wiki/Odin "Odin") leads the hunt (or the similar *Wodenjäger* or *Odinjœgeren*).[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeide201979,_86-114)[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESimek2007180–181,_379–380-108)[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOrchard1997187-20) Old Norse sources also describe Odin and his group in a way that closely resembles almost all aspects of Wild Host traditions, such as his leading of a host of the dead [einherjar](/wiki/Einherjar "Einherjar"), along with [valkyrjur](/wiki/Valkyrjur "Valkyrjur"), who collect the newly dead. Odin is recorded as stealing Yule food in *[Flateyjarbók](/wiki/Flateyjarb%C3%B3k "Flateyjarbók")'*s *[Haralds þáttr hárfagra](/w/index.php?title=Haralds_%C3%BE%C3%A1ttr_h%C3%A1rfagra&action=edit&redlink=1 "Haralds þáttr hárfagra (page does not exist)")*, similar to in later accounts of the host.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeide201988–89-115)[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHaralds_þáttr_hárfagra-116) Furthermore, he is often equated in historical sources with [Mercury](/wiki/Mercury_(mythology) "Mercury (mythology)"), a Roman god who has a role in [guiding the dead to the afterlife](/wiki/Psychopomp "Psychopomp").[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeide201988–89-115) Odin's role as leader of the Wild Host may be the origin of his name, which literally means "lord of frenzy" or "leader of the possessed".[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeide201988-117)[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWest2007137-118)[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELindow200128-26) Similar to Norwegian traditions of leaving out food and drink for the Wild Host, in Southern Scandinavia, the last sheaf cut in harvest or grass during haying would be left for Odin's horse or horses around Christmas.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeide201988–89-115)

#### Guising

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yule&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Guising")]

The supernatural host cannot be separated neatly from the interlinked tradition of hosts of costumed humans travelling the countryside around Yule or Christmas and performing similar acts recorded in the modern period. The ambiguity between the two groups is seen for example in how the supernatural host is sometimes flying in the sky but could also walk along roads as the guised humans would.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeide201978-109) The costumed figures could also be the same as those in the supernatural host, for example the *[julebukk](/wiki/Julebukking "Julebukking")* ("Yule-goat"), in which the top half of the guiser was dressed as a goat. Other figures include the *halmgubbar* ("strawmen"), who were wrapped in straw or corn. Similar to the supernatural host, they often travelled between buildings, variously grunting like animals, singing, dancing and being given food and drink.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunnell199595–105,_112-119) Guisers sometimes would jokingly whip with brushwood those who refused to give them what they demanded.[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeide201982-120) Sometimes these offerings were nearly compulsory, with the visitors not leaving until a "tax" had been paid.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunnell199595–105,_112-119) The figures could also have sexual facets, sometimes having penises as part of the costume and kissing young men and women. In one account from [Trøndelag](/wiki/Tr%C3%B8ndelag "Trøndelag"), the *julbukker* became so angry they butted people with their horns and it was said the first person each one butted was to become their wife.[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunnell1995112-121) These traditions resembles those of the [Sámi](/wiki/S%C3%A1mi "Sámi") [Stallo](/wiki/Stallo "Stallo"), an ugly costumed figure who would poke girls with a stick (sometimes shaped like a penis) until he was paid to leave.[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunnell1995105-122)

The origins of such practices are debated. Some visitations have clear Christian imagery, such as the *Stjärnspel* in which the figures are the [Three Kings](/wiki/Three_Kings "Three Kings") bearing a star, while others lack obvious Christian or foreign influence.[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunnell199595–104-123) The traditions also developed over time, and between regions, with figures such as the *julbukk* never being homogenous throughout Scandinavia.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunnell1995126–127-124) An early attestation of a practice resembling late guising traditions is given in *[Þorleifs þáttur jarlsskálds](/w/index.php?title=%C3%9Eorleifs_%C3%BE%C3%A1ttur_jarlssk%C3%A1lds&action=edit&redlink=1 "Þorleifs þáttur jarlsskálds (page does not exist)")*, found in the late 14th century *[Flateyjarbók](/wiki/Flateyjarb%C3%B3k "Flateyjarbók")* manuscript. It tells how one Yule in heathen times, the skald Þorleifur visits the Norwegian ruler [Hákon Jarl](/w/index.php?title=H%C3%A1kon_Jarl&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hákon Jarl (page does not exist)") at a feast to get revenge on him for a previous insult. To avoid being recognised, he goes disguised as a food beggar, wearing a goat beard, placing a leather bag behind it, and using crutches such that he walked on all four legs. When the jarl invites him to eat, he secretly puts the food into the leather bag rather than his mouth, before saying a ritual curse to Hákon which makes him lose hair from his head.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunnell2007284-125) Though the disguise traditions are typically attested late, they do closely resemble visitations by figures such as *[Grýla](/wiki/Gr%C3%BDla "Grýla")*, [groleks](/w/index.php?title=Groleks&action=edit&redlink=1 "Groleks (page does not exist)") and [skeklers](/w/index.php?title=Skeklers&action=edit&redlink=1 "Skeklers (page does not exist)") that may in turn have roots heathen ritual dramas and are found across a large geographical area, including Shetland, Iceland and Sweden. Similar to the *julebukk*, people dressed as these figures sometimes spoke in reverse speech, in which sound is made while breathing in, and could have a wooden penis as part of the costume. Notably, *Grýla* is attested in 13th century sources such as the [Prose Edda](/wiki/Prose_Edda "Prose Edda")'s [þulr](/wiki/Nafna%C3%BEulur "Nafnaþulur") of [troll](/wiki/Troll "Troll")-women, *[Íslendinga saga](/wiki/%C3%8Dslendinga_saga "Íslendinga saga")* and *[Sverris saga](/wiki/Sverris_saga "Sverris saga")*.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunnell200133–34-126) Comparisons have also been drawn between these customs, particularly those of straw figures, and [Freyr](/wiki/Freyr "Freyr"), who in *[Gunnars þáttr helmings](/wiki/Gunnars_%C3%BE%C3%A1ttr_helmings "Gunnars þáttr helmings")* travels around Sweden in winter between farms, partaking in feasts and ensuring good harvests.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunnell199595–105-127)[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcKinnell1987142-128)

## Academic reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yule&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Academic reception")]

### Significance and connection to other events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yule&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Significance and connection to other events")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Die_Gartenlaube_%281880%29_b_856.jpg/330px-Die_Gartenlaube_%281880%29_b_856.jpg)](/wiki/File:Die_Gartenlaube_(1880)_b_856.jpg)

Illustration of an ancient Nordic Yule festival (*[Die Gartenlaube](/wiki/Die_Gartenlaube "Die Gartenlaube")*, 1880)

As early as the 1600s, researchers identified Yule as a solstice festival, possibly being a feast for the newborn sun. By the 1890s, this had become generally accepted among scholars. This theory became challenged later on, particularly through the belief that the pre-Christian Germanic peoples were not culturally sophisticated enough to know about solstices. Part of the reasoning for this is that 'solstice' is a Latin word. Germanic terms seemingly independent of Latin do seem to exist, however, including *sólhverf* ("sun-turning") and *solhof* ("sun-heightening"), and it is now widely accepted that solstices were known about by these peoples before Christianisation.[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENordberg2006100–101-129) However, the evidence has been interpreted as indicating variation by region and period, rather than a single uniform heathen midwinter festival fixed exclusively to the solstice date.[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETurvillePetre196428–30-130)[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrice2014102–104-131) More recently, Yule has been variously argued by scholars to be part of the cult of the dead, a fertility festival and a new year festival.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESimek2007379–380-25)[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESommer2018-132)[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOrchard1997187-20) Interpretations typically draw attention to the liminality of the time, including the threat from supernatural forces, and the prominent darkness in the middle of winter.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESimek2007379–380-25)[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESommer2018-132) In Scandinavian contexts, midwinter observance is described as being shaped by environmental constraints and risk management, including reliance on stored provisions, restricted mobility, and vulnerability of livestock and households.[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHedeager2011142–145-133)[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrink200875–78-134) Scholarly discussion has also emphasised the role of midwinter customs in reinforcing household order and social cohesion during prolonged indoor confinement.[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrice201495–101-135)

The [Yule log](/wiki/Yule_log "Yule log"), Yule boar, Yule singing, and other late customs may have connections to pre-Christian Yule traditions and have been proposed by [Simek](/wiki/Simek "Simek") to indicate the importance of the festival before Christianisation.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESimek2007379–380-25)

### Existence of pre-Christian Yule

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yule&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Existence of pre-Christian Yule")]

British historian [Ronald Hutton](/wiki/Ronald_Hutton "Ronald Hutton") wrote of the term "Yule" that there is "doubt over whether it was originally attached to a midwinter festival which preceded the Christian one [of Christmas]".[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton19966-136) It is suggested that the [Vikings](/wiki/Vikings "Vikings") who settled in England introduced or popularised 'Yule' as a name for Christmas among the Anglo-Saxons.[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton19966-136)[[48]](#cite_note-Parker-50) The Gothic form of 'Yule' is, however, attested in a calendar from c. 350 CE, while Bede writes it in its Old English form in *The Reckoning of Time*, written in the 8th century.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENordberg200621,_147–148-37) Hutton further writes that the earliest [Scandinavian literature](/wiki/Scandinavian_literature "Scandinavian literature"), before Snorri, makes no reference to Yule as a pagan feast.[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton19966-136) The typical view in scholarship is that Yule was indeed a heathen winter feast in [North Germanic communities](/wiki/North_Germanic_peoples "North Germanic peoples") that was later applied to Christmas as Christianity became established.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJól-17)[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESundqvist2015322–323-137)[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESimek2007180–181,_379–380-108)[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDriscoll199582-24) Yule feasts feature extensively in Old Norse texts such as *[Heimskringla](/wiki/Heimskringla "Heimskringla")*, written in the early 13th century and based on earlier sources. These include *Haraldskvæði*, composed around 900 CE in praise of a heathen king who "drinks Yule".[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESundqvist2015320–321,_352-138)[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeimskringlavii–xivIntroduction,_Hálfdanar_saga_svarta,_chapters_5_&_8,_Hákonar_saga_goðar,_chapter_18-139)

It has further been stressed that, even disregarding all speculation about the nature of pre-Christian Yule celebrations, it is very unlikely that Germanic peoples did not have seasonal or midwinter festivals when Christian missionaries arrived.[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunnell2000126-140)

## Contemporary traditions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yule&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Contemporary traditions")]

### Relationship with Christmas in Northern Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yule&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Relationship with Christmas in Northern Europe")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Chambers_Yule_Log.png/250px-Chambers_Yule_Log.png)](/wiki/File:Chambers_Yule_Log.png)

Hauling a Yule log in 1832.

As Christianity became established among Germanic peoples, Yule slowly merged with the Christian festival of Christmas.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOrchard1997187-20) [Ágrip](/wiki/%C3%81grip "Ágrip"), for example, tells that [Ólaf Tryggvason](/wiki/%C3%93laf_Tryggvason "Ólaf Tryggvason") Christianised Norway, Iceland, [Shetland](/wiki/Shetland "Shetland"), [Orkney](/wiki/Orkney "Orkney") and the [Faroes](/wiki/Faroes "Faroes") in the late 10th century. In the account, he banned *[blót](/wiki/Bl%C3%B3t "Blót")* and *blót*-drinking, replacing these with holiday feasts at Yule, Easter and ale at St John's Mass and Michaelmas.[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunnell2000123-141)[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDriscoll199530–33-142) Similarly, [Hákon the Good](/wiki/H%C3%A1kon_the_Good "Hákon the Good"), according to *[Hákonar saga góða](/wiki/H%C3%A1konar_saga_g%C3%B3%C3%B0a "Hákonar saga góða")*, passed a law in Norway to make Yule celebrations take place at the same time as the Christians celebrated Christmas.[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHollander2007106-143) These actions have been compared to [Pope Gregory](/wiki/Gregory_the_Great "Gregory the Great")'s recommendation in 601 to [Augustine](/wiki/Augustine_of_Canterbury "Augustine of Canterbury") to convert the heathen English by renaming existing traditional practices and taking over holy sites rather than trying to eradicate them.[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunnell2000123-141) In England, Yule was used to refer to Christmas by the second half of the 9th century, where it is attested in the [Doom book](/wiki/Doom_book "Doom book") of [Alfred the Great](/wiki/Alfred_the_Great "Alfred the Great") (c. 890) as *gehhol*, and the [Old English Martyrology](/wiki/Old_English_Martyrology "Old English Martyrology") (c. 800-900) as *geolum*.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOED_Online2022-1)[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPreston2014121-144)[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERauer20131–2-145) Some later Old English texts call Christmas Day *Geohel-dæg* (Yule Day).[[141]](#cite_note-146)

Today, variants of "Yule" are the main names for Christmas in the [North Germanic languages](/wiki/North_Germanic_languages "North Germanic languages") as well as in the [Finnic languages](/wiki/Finnic_languages "Finnic languages"). "Yule" and "Yuletide" are alternative names for Christmas and Christmastide in English. Traditionally, *Yule* or *Yuil* is also the main name for Christmas in [Scots](/wiki/Scots_language "Scots language").[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton199626–28-147)

### Modern paganism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yule&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Modern paganism")]

As [contemporary pagan religions](/wiki/Modern_Paganism "Modern Paganism") differ in both origin and practice, these representations of Yule can vary considerably despite the shared name. Some [Heathens](/wiki/Heathenry_(new_religious_movement) "Heathenry (new religious movement)"), for example, celebrate in a way as close as possible to how they believe [ancient Germanic pagans](/wiki/Germanic_paganism "Germanic paganism") observed the tradition, while others observe the holiday with rituals "assembled from different sources".[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHutton2008-148)

In most forms of [Wicca](/wiki/Wicca "Wicca"), this holiday is celebrated at the [winter solstice](/wiki/Winter_solstice "Winter solstice") as the rebirth of the [Great horned hunter god](/wiki/Horned_God "Horned God"),[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBuescher2007-149) who is viewed as the newborn solstice sun. The method of gathering for this [sabbat](/wiki/Wheel_of_the_Year#Festivals "Wheel of the Year") varies by practitioner. Some have private ceremonies at home,[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKannapell1997-150) while others do so with their [covens](/wiki/Coven "Coven"):

> Generally meeting in covens, which anoint their own priests and priestesses, Wiccans chant and cast or draw circles to invoke their deities, mainly during festivals like Samhain and Yule, which coincide with Halloween and Christmas, and when the moon is full.[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELa_Ferla2000-151)

### LaVeyan Satanism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yule&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: LaVeyan Satanism")]

Some members of the [Church of Satan](/wiki/Church_of_Satan "Church of Satan") and other [LaVeyan Satanist](/wiki/LaVeyan_Satanism "LaVeyan Satanism") groups celebrate Yule at the same time as the Christian holiday in a secular manner.[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEscobedo2015-152)

* [Yaldā Night](/wiki/Yald%C4%81_Night "Yaldā Night"), an Iranian festival celebrated on the "longest and darkest night of the year".
* [Nardoqan](/wiki/Nardoqan "Nardoqan"), the birth of the sun, is an ancient Turkic festival that celebrates the winter solstice.

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Yule&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-34)** Shown text reads: "...nath, Oktober Vuinter-fylleth, November Blod-monath, December Giuli, eodem Januarius nomine, vocatur. Incipiebant autem annum ab octavo Calendarum Januariarum die, ubi nunc..." ("...nath; October, Winterfilleth; November, Blodmonath; December, Giuli, the same name by which January is called. They began the year on the 8th kalends of January, when...")[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBedeChapter_15._The_English_Months-33)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-47)** The translated text reads "... a very wonderful thing takes place each year. For the sun at the time of the summer solstice never sets for forty days, but appears constantly during this whole time above the earth. But no less than six months later, at about the time of the winter solstice, the sun is never seen on this island for forty days, but never-ending night envelops it; and as a result of this dejection holds the people there during this whole time, because they are unable by any means to mingle with one another during this interval... And when a time amounting to thirty-five days has passed in this long night, certain men are sent to the summits of the mountains - for this is the custom among them - and when they are able from that point barely to see the sun, they bring back word to the people below that within five days the sun will shine upon them. And the whole population celebrates a festival at the good news, and that too in the darkness. And this is the greatest festival which the natives of Thule have; for, I imagine, these islanders always become terrified, although they see the same thing happen each year, fearing that the sun may at some time fail them entirely."[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGunnell2000121–122-46)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-66)** This kenning is used in Grani skald's praise of [Haraldr Sigurðarson](/wiki/Haraldr_Sigur%C3%B0arson "Haraldr Sigurðarson"), quoted in the [Morkinskinna](/wiki/Morkinskinna "Morkinskinna") and [Hulda-Hrokkinskinna](/wiki/Hulda-Hrokkinskinna "Hulda-Hrokkinskinna") versions of [Skáldskaparmál](/wiki/Sk%C3%A1ldskaparm%C3%A1l "Skáldskaparmál"). An alternative translation of the phrase containing *hugins jól* is "I believe the ruler prepared a yule-feast for the retinue of Huginn [raven]", which carries the same overall meaning.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrani_Har_2II-65)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-75)** The evidence for the connection between ceremonial drinking and pre-Christian Germanic culture at times other than Yule is extensive, as seen in depictions in *[Beowulf](/wiki/Beowulf "Beowulf")*, the invitation of Hákon the Good to drink with the gods in *[Hákonarmál](/wiki/H%C3%A1konarm%C3%A1l "Hákonarmál")*, and a [lausavisa](/wiki/Lausavisa "Lausavisa") composed by *[Egils saga](/wiki/Egils_saga "Egils saga")* that scorns the lack of beer at a [dísablót](/wiki/D%C3%ADsabl%C3%B3t "Dísablót").[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESundqvist2015353–354,_361-74)
5. **[^](#cite_ref-95)** The identification of the dancers with Varangians is supported by *De officiis*, written in the mid 1300s by [Georgios Kodinos](/w/index.php?title=Georgios_Kodinos&action=edit&redlink=1 "Georgios Kodinos (page does not exist)") as an expansion and accompaniment to the *Book of ceremonies*. It records how at the emperor's Christmas feast, Varangians would wish him well in their native tongue (English) and make noise by hitting their axes together. [[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESandin2009126-94)
